title: I had Claude break down the 5 biggest winners my EURUSD AI ran end-to-end. Here's what they had in common. (67% Win Ratio)
author: The_AI_Trader
selftext:
Wanted to share an analysis I had Claude do on the top trades my EURUSD AI model has taken, breaking them down to see what we can actually learn from them. This is a multi agentic AI platform, that uses multiple agents to take on the trades. There are more AI Traders, in other markets. But this is only the EURUSD. I asked Claude to go into each trade, look at the analysis, all the agents outputs, and all the details we have on the trades.

**This is what Claude wrote:**

Let me start with the baseline — the headline performance across all 15 trades — so the deep-dive on the top 5 winners has something to be measured against.

For context: this model has analyzed 229 sessions and taken 15 trades only (it filters hard — about 6.5% of sessions clear the bar and become trades). Performance per TP target:

\- TP1: 67% win rate, avg 1.1R  
\- TP2: 53% win rate, avg 1.7R  
\- TP3: 40% win rate, avg 2.5R

Solid numbers on a small sample. Rather than just present the headline stats, I pulled the pre-trade analysis on the 5 biggest R-multiple winners — all hit TP3 — to see what they had in common that the rest of the trades didn't. Five EURUSD intraday TP3 winners. R-multiples 1.81 to 4.20. Four shorts, one long. The most interesting trade isn't the +4.20R short — it's the long: the Macro Agent said bearish (15% conviction), the Trend Agent said bullish (67%), and the system overrode macro and printed +3.19R. Cherry-picked by construction — these are the winners. The point is seeing what reasoning the AI committed to before price moved, and which patterns repeat across the top of the distribution.

A quick word on the agents involved, since you'll see them named throughout. The Macro Agent picks a group bias (e.g. all FX majors) and a per-instrument slice inside that group. The Trend Agent runs per instrument and outputs direction, confidence, and regime (TRENDING, TRANSITIONING, etc.). The Entry Agent stacks both with chart structure and produces a numbered setup with explicit confluences, a conditional trigger, and a TP ladder where every TP has a structural reason — never "1R/2R/3R."

**THE SCORECARD**

  \#   Date     Dir    R     Setup type              Model  
  1   Apr 23   SHORT  4.20  Resistance rejection    Opus 4.6  
  2   Mar 26   SHORT  3.34  VWAP rally fade         Opus 4.6  
  3   Apr 13   LONG   3.19  Pullback to structure   Opus 4.6  
  4   May 13   SHORT  1.92  VWAP/Fib fade           Opus 4.6  
  5   Mar 18   SHORT  1.81  Pre-FOMC rally fade     Opus 4.6

**WHAT THESE 5 TRADES SHARED**

If you only have a minute, this is the takeaway. The trade-by-trade below lets you check whether each pattern actually holds — I'd rather you stress-test these than take my word for it.

**1. SAME 5-PIECE CONFLUENCE STACK EVERY TIME:** DXY vs 5-day EMA, plus 10Y yield direction, plus 60m EMA stack, plus a structural level (VWAP / Fib / session S/R), plus a clean calendar. Boring base recipe, load-bearing on every trade.

**2. EVERY TP3 ANCHORED TO CHART STRUCTURE** — session low, 60m pivot, psychological round — never "3R from entry." This is why the runners ran.

**3. STOP SIZE DOMINATED R OUTCOMES MORE THAN HOW FAR PRICE MOVED.** Tight conditional entries (7.5–13 pip stops) produced the biggest R; wider stops (14–18 pips) capped R even on the trade with the strongest macro signal of the five.

**4. WHEN MACRO AGENT AND TREND AGENT DISAGREED, CONFIDENCE WEIGHTING WON**. Not a hard rule like "trend beats macro" — whichever agent had higher conviction got the weight. One trade in the set shows this clearly.

Now the trades.

**==================================================**  
**TRADE #1 — SHORT EURUSD — R=4.20 — 2026-04-23 (Opus 4.6)**  
**==================================================**

Setup: Conditional Short EURUSD at Resistance Rejection

Biggest R of the set. Macro group was strong\_bull USD at 52%, but the EURUSD slice inside the group was already strong\_bear -78 at 63% conviction. Narrative: Iran ceasefire collapse → DXY +52.9bp in 48h to 98.563, German GDP cut from 1.0% to 0.5%, US 10Y holding 4.296%. Trend Agent: bearish 66%, regime TRANSITIONING, MODERATE strength.

Note the TRANSITIONING regime — the AI flagged it as not a clean trend, which is what shaped the conditional entry structure rather than a market entry.

Confluences:  
\- Macro bias bearish (\~70–75%)  
\- Trend Agent bearish (66%)  
\- DXY above 5D EMA and rising  
\- 10Y yields rising  
\- 60m EMA stack bearish  
\- Calendar clear

Risks the AI called out:  
\- Counter-trend squeeze in progress  
\- TRANSITIONING regime recommends reduced size  
\- Breakout above 1.17133 would invalidate  
\- VWAP/support could slow downside

Entry trigger was conditional, not a touch-and-go:

  "Enter only if price tags the 1.17080-1.17130 resistance cluster and prints  
   a 5m rejection signal (bearish engulfing, pin bar/doji with upper wick,  
   or stall with 5m RSI turning back below 60). If price closes and holds  
   above 1.17133 on a 5m close, the setup is invalidated."

TP ladder, each level a structural reason:  
\- TP1 1.1698 — VWAP / 1R reaction zone  
\- TP2 1.16928 — Trend Agent support / prior structure  
\- TP3 1.16792 — Session low continuation target

Outcome: entered 1.17105, stop 1.1718, exited 1.1679 at TP3. R = 4.20. \~2.5h.

Why it ran: macro and structure agreed, but the AI didn't trust a TRANSITIONING regime enough to market-short. It required price to come to the level AND prove rejection. Tight 7.5-pip stop + structural TP3 target = biggest R in the set.

**==================================================**  
**TRADE #2 — SHORT EURUSD — R=3.34 — 2026-03-26 (Opus 4.6)**  
**==================================================**

Setup: EURUSD SHORT (Sell the Rally to VWAP)

Macro fully stacked. Forex group strong\_bear 62%, EURUSD slice strong\_bear -78 at 55%. Headline was structural USD momentum from hawkish Fed inflation repricing (DXY 99.82, +1.69% MTD). VIX 26.68 in high\_vol regime — pulled position-size multiplier down to 0.65 (the system literally cut size in high vol). Trend Agent: bearish 62%, TRANSITIONING, WEAK.

Confluences:  
\- Trend Agent bearish at 62% confidence  
\- DXY above 5d EMA and rising  
\- 10Y yields rising, bearish EURUSD  
\- 60m EMA stack bearish (price < fast < slow)  
\- Entry at VWAP + session resistance + Fibonacci cluster on 5m  
\- Calendar clear — no events within 30 min

Risks the AI called out:  
\- Corrective bounce momentum may extend through VWAP and toward 1.15646  
\- Trend Agent regime is TRANSITIONING with reduced size guidance  
\- VIX-driven whipsaws around VWAP  
\- Trump speech later in the day may support EURUSD temporarily  
\- 5m close above 1.15660 invalidates the setup

Trigger:

  "Price touches 1.15540–1.15555 and prints a 5m rejection candle  
   (upper wick > body) OR fails to close above VWAP on two consecutive  
   5m candles."

TP ladder:  
\- TP1 1.1539 — session support / 60m candle structure  
\- TP2 1.15235 — session low / Trend Agent support  
\- TP3 1.151 — psychological level / extrapolated extension

Outcome: entered 1.15531, stop 1.1566, exited 1.151 at TP3. R = 3.34. Overnight hold (\~20h).

Why it ran: entry into a counter-trend bounce AT a VWAP/Fib/resistance triple confluence with macro and rates aligned. Notable: the AI used the bounce itself as the entry zone rather than fading from below — waited for the rally and shorted it instead of trying to short the trend continuation. Better fill, smaller stop, larger R.

**==================================================**  
**TRADE #3 — LONG EURUSD — R=3.19 — 2026-04-13 (Opus 4.6)**  
**==================================================**

Setup: EURUSD Pullback Buy at Structure

The most analytically interesting trade in the set. Macro did NOT say long. Forex group lean\_bear 28%, EURUSD slice lean\_bear -18 at only 15% conviction. The AI took a long because Trend Agent flipped the table — bullish 67% in TRENDING regime, MODERATE strength. When Trend Agent reads TRENDING and macro lean is weak, the system weights structure over macro. Macro doesn't appear in the confluence list at all — that's not an oversight, it's the weighting.

Confluences:  
\- Trend Agent bullish at 67% in TRENDING regime  
\- DXY below 5-day EMA and declining, supporting EURUSD  
\- 60m EMA stack bullish with expanding positive MACD histogram  
\- Entry zone aligns with Trend Agent support, 5d EMA, 5m Fib 78.6%, and psychological 1.17000  
\- 15m RSI at 61.5 confirms bullish bias without being overbought  
\- No high-impact calendar events

Risks the AI called out (notable — it explicitly acknowledged it was leaning against macro):  
\- Rising 10Y yields remain a bearish EURUSD headwind  
\- LTF RSI was overbought at the highs and may indicate near-term exhaustion  
\- Price is extended above VWAP, increasing pullback risk  
\- Macro backdrop is only mildly bullish, not strongly supportive

Four references stacking at one price: Trend Agent support, 5d EMA, 5m Fib 78.6%, round 1.17000. Textbook pullback-into-structure and rare to get all four aligned.

Trigger:

  "Bullish reaction candle on the 5m after price tests the 1.17000–1.17050  
   zone; alternatively, a 5m candle reclaiming 1.17118 on a shallower  
   pullback."

TP ladder — every level a 60m structural reference:  
\- TP1 1.1717 — 60m resistance / today's HOD retest zone  
\- TP2 1.1723 — 60m resistance cluster  
\- TP3 1.17384 — 60m pivot high / major structural target

Outcome: entered 1.17047, stop 1.1694, exited 1.17388 at TP3. R = 3.19. \~3h.

Why it ran: the per-instrument Trend Agent (67%, TRENDING) was higher conviction than the macro group bias (lean\_bear, 15%). The Entry Agent listens to whoever's more confident. When macro is genuinely uncertain, local structure wins. Important counterfactual: if macro had been -78 at 70%+ conviction, the long doesn't get taken. The system isn't ignoring macro, it's weighting it.

**==================================================**  
**TRADE #4 — SHORT EURUSD — R=1.92 — 2026-05-13 (GPT-5.5)**  
**==================================================**

Setup: EURUSD Short: VWAP / Fib Rejection Fade

The only GPT-5.5 trade in the set. Worth comparing.

Strongest macro signal of all five. Forex group strong\_bull 78% (highest), EURUSD slice strong\_bear -82 at 79% conviction (also highest). Trigger was a major PPI beat:

  "USD strength accelerates following massive PPI beats (Core 1.0% vs 0.3%,  
   Headline 1.4% vs 0.5%), eliminating Fed cut probability through Q3 2026  
   while parliamentary-mandated rate cuts lock in policy divergence across  
   GBP and CAD."

Trend Agent: bearish 65%, TRANSITIONING, MODERATE.

Confluences (note: GPT-5.5 explicitly labels the agent sources — Opus tends to hide this):  
\- Macro Agent bearish on EURUSD with 76% confidence  
\- Trend Agent bearish with 65% confidence  
\- DXY above 5-day EMA and rising  
\- US 10Y yield rising, supporting EURUSD downside  
\- 60m EMA structure bearish  
\- Price at 5m VWAP / Fib / session resistance

Risks the AI called out (includes a hard rule that prohibits the opposite direction — Opus doesn't surface this in its risk blocks):  
\- 60m RSI recently oversold, making follow-through potentially choppy  
\- Trend regime is transitioning, suggesting reduced size  
\- 5m momentum has bounced, so premature entry risks failure  
\- Tentative Fed nomination vote creates headline risk  
\- Longs are explicitly prohibited by the macro hard rule

GPT-5.5's trigger is more procedural than the Opus triggers — five stacked conditions, including a time-gate:

  "Valid only after 10:05am ET. Enter short only if price tests  
   1.17090–1.17110, a 5-minute candle rejects VWAP/Fib resistance and  
   closes back below 1.17090, 15-minute RSI rolls back below 50 or fails  
   to hold above 50, price remains below 1.17216–1.17220, and no fresh  
   bullish EURUSD headline or sudden DXY reversal occurs."

TP ladder:  
\- TP1 1.16966 — Today's low / London low support  
\- TP2 1.16885 — Downside extension below session low  
\- TP3 1.1683 — Full continuation target if USD strength persists

Outcome: entered 1.17085, stop 1.17225, exited 1.16816 at TP3. R = 1.92. Overnight hold (\~24.5h).

Why R is smaller despite the strongest macro signal: GPT-5.5 used a 14-pip stop. Opus shorts in this set ran 7.5–13 pip stops. Same TP3 hit, wider stop, smaller R.

**==================================================**  
**TRADE #5 — SHORT EURUSD — R=1.81 — 2026-03-18 (Opus 4.6)**  
**==================================================**

Setup: EURUSD SHORT rally fade into VWAP/resistance

Pre-FOMC. Notable that the AI took the trade into an event window at all. Macro group bear 64%, EURUSD strong\_bear -72 at 72%. Narrative: Strait of Hormuz escalation → oil $105.95 → energy-importer (EUR, GBP) fiscal burden → death-cross confirmed on the EURUSD daily. Trend Agent bearish ≥60% (older snapshot, doesn't capture regime/strength fields).

Confluences:  
\- Macro Agent bearish, confidence >= 60  
\- Trend Agent bearish, confidence >= 60  
\- 10Y yield trend supports short bias  
\- 60m structure supports bearish pullback: price below fast EMA, MACD histogram negative  
\- 5m entry zone aligns with VWAP / fib / intraday resistance  
\- 15m RSI is below 50 / near 48-49, not extreme  
\- No high-impact event within 30 minutes

Risks the AI called out:  
\- DXY is not fully aligned with the short thesis  
\- 5m momentum has stabilized and is no longer impulsively bearish  
\- Midday liquidity can produce chop and false breaks  
\- FOMC later today can suppress clean follow-through

Trigger:

  "5m rejection candle or failed reclaim at/just below VWAP, ideally after  
   tagging 5m fib resistance / intraday VWAP band and closing back below  
   1.15230"

TP ladder (older record, no per-level descriptions):  
\- TP1 1.15098  
\- TP2 1.15043  
\- TP3 1.1491

Outcome: entered 1.15232, stop 1.1541, exited 1.1491 at TP3. R = 1.81. \~3h. Hit TP3 before FOMC fired.

Why R is smaller: macro was strong enough to take a rally fade into event risk, but the 17.8-pip stop was the widest of the set — wide enough to absorb pre-event chop. Same TP3-hit-but-smaller-R dynamic as Trade #4.

# ==================================================THE FOUR PATTERNS AGAIN — WITH THE RECEIPTS

Now that you've seen the trades, here's the long-form version of each pattern with the specific data points behind it. Same four patterns from the top, expanded.

1. MACRO AND TREND AGREED IN FOUR OF FIVE — THE OUTLIER SHOWS THE WEIGHTING LOGIC. Trade #3 went long when macro said lean\_bear at 15% and Trend Agent said bullish 67% TRENDING. The Entry Agent didn't apply a rule like "trend beats macro" — it weighted by confidence. Macro at 15% is functionally uncertainty. Trend at 67% in a TRENDING regime is a real signal. Flip macro to -78 at 70% and that long doesn't happen.
2. THE CONFLUENCE STACK WAS IDENTICAL EVERY TIME. If there's one takeaway from this audit, it's the recipe:

  DXY vs 5-day EMA + 10Y yield direction + 60m EMA stack  
  \+ a structural level (VWAP / Fib / session S/R) + clean calendar.

That's the base across all five. Trend Agent regime/strength and macro narrative layer on top. None of the five skipped any of the base ingredients. The boring 5-ingredient recipe was load-bearing.

3. EVERY TP3 HAD A STRUCTURAL REASON, NEVER A FIXED-R NUMBER. "Session low continuation." "Psychological level / extrapolated extension." "60m pivot high / major structural target." None were "3R from entry." This is the most copy-able idea here — most retail traders set TPs at fixed R multiples. The AI anchors them to where price would HAVE A REASON to stop. That's why the top of the distribution runs as far as it does.

4. STOP SIZE DOMINATED R OUTCOMES — NOT HOW FAR PRICE MOVED. The biggest-R trades (#1 4.20, #2 3.34) had 7.5–13 pip stops from tight conditional entries. The smaller-R trades (#4 1.92, #5 1.81) had 14–18 pip stops, even though #4 had the strongest macro signal of all five. Same TP3 hit, wildly different R. Tighter entries — not larger market moves — did the heavy lifting on the upside. The conditional-entry discipline (wait for the rejection candle, don't market-enter) shows up directly in the R distribution.

HONEST CAVEATS

\- n=5, filtered to the largest-R TP3 winners by construction. Survivorship bias is baked in. There are TP2-only exits, breakeven stops, and full stops on this same model. Happy to post the loss distribution next if there's interest — that's honestly the more useful read.  
\- AI confidence numbers are self-reported. "Trend Agent bearish at 66%" is the agent's own score. Calibrating that against ground truth across thousands of trades is a different analysis.

THE QUESTION I WANT ANSWERED

When your macro read and your local trend disagree, do you skip the trade, or weight by confidence the way Trade #3 did?

Skipping is disciplined but misses real trades. Weighting is right in theory but hard to encode as a hard rule — and the moment you write the rule, the next edge case breaks it. Curious how the discretionary folks here handle this vs. the systematic folks.

comments.author: SmartWeb2711
comments.body: Can you share your setup ? Interested to test
